what_it_does: A read-only scan of /home/tim/company that inventories the existing registry, graph-MCP, and routing substrates and argues that a reusable trigger/hook system can be built by binding three already-existing halves: an event-kind taxonomy, a RULE_OPS predicate language, and a DESTINATION_KINDS routing layer.
observations: A trigger row is defined as event-kind → when-predicate → action.
The three required halves are said to already exist in activation.py, rules.py, and rules.py route() respectively; only the binding registry is missing.
No repo-root directory or class named trigger/hook/event/listener/watch/signal exists.
mode_detection_rules.py is identified as the strongest structural template for a file-discovered, RULE_OPS-based, priority-ordered registry.
rules.py:65 RULE_OPS, :114 DESTINATION_KINDS, :130 FORBIDDEN_DESTINATION_VERBS, and :491 route() are treated as the core reuse targets.
activation.py:64-106 ACTIVATION_CONTEXTS supplies the event-kind taxonomy (turn/idle-loop/sense/timer).
The registry system has Tier A (discovered-and-wired in suite.py __init__) and Tier B (_CORPUS_REGISTRIES, MCP-writable) wiring.
A trigger row is pure data, so it is inferred to be Tier-B-eligible for create(kind='trigger') authoring.
MCP tools live in mcp_face/tools/*.py and are auto-discovered by pkgutil; a new triggers.py tool module would fit this pattern.
The doc ends with open questions for Tim about lifting the event taxonomy and whether triggers may fire graphs.
standouts: The central finding that a trigger system is a unification of existing parts, not a net-new mechanism.
mode_detection_rules.py is singled out as the strongest structural template to clone.
rules.route() + DESTINATION_KINDS is named as the single most important reuse target.
Despite event→action mechanisms in activation.py and mode_detection_rules.py, no first-class trigger registry exists.
conventions: Follows the repo's registry-is-truth law: file-discovered <name>/ dirs, self-registering <id>.py files, id==filename, fail-loud _build_* gate, rediscover(), AGENTS.md drift-home.
Uses an evidence-classification scheme: Observed, Inferred, sub-agent-reported.
Distinguishes Tier A (suite __init__ discovery) from Tier B (_CORPUS_REGISTRIES MCP-writable) registry wiring.
Honors the floor constraint that rules/triggers cannot use FORBIDDEN_DESTINATION_VERBS (resolve/approve/dispatch).
concerns: Several line spans and function bodies are marked sub-agent-reported rather than personally verified.
The Tier-B eligibility claim is explicitly caveated because mode_detection_rules.py is pure-data yet absent from _CORPUS_REGISTRIES, leaving the real gating reason unresolved.
The report leaves multiple design decisions unresolved and delegates them to Tim.
The 'no trigger registry exists' conclusion depends on naming-based grep and directory listing, which could miss conceptually similar systems under other names.
notes: A pre-implementation scan arguing the codebase already contains the ingredients for a trigger system and only needs a binding registry plus a thin driver.
questions: Why is mode_detection_rules.py pure-data but still excluded from _CORPUS_REGISTRIES?
Should the event-kind taxonomy in ACTIVATION_CONTEXTS be lifted into its own file-discovered registry?
Should triggers be restricted to DESTINATION_KINDS, or allowed to fire graphs via run_graph?
Which sub-agent-reported line numbers need personal verification before implementation?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to scan registries, graph-MCP, and routing for a reusable trigger/hook system; the file does exactly that.
apparent_themes: Unification of existing subsystems rather than building anew.
Registry-is-truth and file-discovery as the canonical extension pattern.
Reuse-before-fork, especially for RULE_OPS and rules.route().
Floor constraints that prevent triggers from resolving, approving, or dispatching.
intention_signals: To justify and blueprint a first-class trigger registry by demonstrating that every hard piece already exists in the codebase and identifying only the missing binding and driver.
novelty: It reframes a trigger/hook system not as a new engine but as a missing registry row that joins event taxonomy, predicate language, and routing destinations that are already separate subsystems.
contribution: Provides a consolidated inventory of existing event, predicate, and routing subsystems plus a concrete build shape for a trigger registry, so a fused system can reuse RULE_OPS and rules.route() instead of duplicating them.
summary_for_embedding: A read-only codebase scan arguing a reusable trigger/hook system can be assembled by unifying the existing event taxonomy, RULE_OPS predicate language, and rules.route destination layer into a file-discovered, MCP-exposed trigger registry.
intention_for_embedding: To map existing registry, graph-MCP, and routing substrates so a trigger system can be built from current code with minimal new machinery.
